reasoning: D and C both correctly diagnose the substituted-question trap (footfall is a proxy, not the goal) and separate the two stocks of people-in-building vs value-delivered, which is the core structural insight the prompt rewards; both attach a value-delivered metric and a premortem to each direction. D edges ahead on usefulness by giving a crisp paradigm-vs-parameter (Meadows) frame, a baseline-freeze and premortem testing protocol, and a concrete this-quarter verdict naming which two directions structurally protect the budget. C is nearly equal, with the sharpest calibration ('ranked priors, held loosely', warning that early enthusiasm may feed direction 4) and an explicit adversarial black-hat on the third-space day-shelter risk. B is strong and well-organized around three 'monopolies' with caveats and a clear single best bet, but treats footfall less critically and does less to challenge whether declining visits are even the right problem. A is solid and actionable with sensible tests and uncertainty notes, but it is the most first-order: it asserts reframings without surfacing the proxy/denominator trap or steelmanning alternatives, so it ranks last.
